coredata.dc:title: Enhanced detection of glucose with carbon quantum dot-modified copper oxide: Computational insight and machine learning modeling of electrochemical sensing

coredata.dc:description: Poor conductivity and surface passivation pose critical challenges in metal oxide structures during their application for non-enzymatic oxidation. To address this, we systematically employed in-situ deposition of carbon-quantum dots (C-dots) over copper oxide (CuO), enhancing its electrocatalytic properties for direct non-enzymatic glucose oxidation in alkaline media. The process involved the systematic deposition of varying wt.% of C-dots onto the CuO nanostructure. The electrode’s sensing capability was assessed through CV, DPV, and amperometric measurements, evaluating its suitability in high (0.1 to 0.85 mM) and low glucose concentration levels (15 to 225 nM) with a representative LOD of 1.4 nM (17142.86 µA mM−1 cm−2). Additionally, the CuO-Cdot-16.6 protective coating allowed for long-term working capability, with chronoamperometric measurement confirming a 99 % current retention ability compared to pristine CuO’s 39 % retention during 3500 s of continuous measurement. DFT calculations further confirmed the efficacy of CuO substrate as a scaffold for glucose adsorption. The stable CuO-glucose complex formed due to energetically favorable conditions further strengthens its potential as a sensor. Successful recoveries of spiked glucose serum samples validated the sensor’s practical usage in complex matrices. Moreover, Machine learning was also adopted to validate the accuracy of glucose detection, where artificial neural network (ANN) model emerged as a suitable model to interpret the DPV derived data relationships, adding in sensor working capability and promising its future application in precision/intelligent healthcare.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing synthesis of CuO NSs and their utilization as scaffold for in-situ growth of C-dots and their application in enhanced electrochemical oxidation of glucose as depicted by improved CV oxidation current.

HR-SEM images of (a) pristine CuO, (b-c) CuO-Cdots-16.6, and magnified image (d) depicting deposition of C-dots over CuO sheets, (e) top-view of the nanosheets, and (f) EDS mapping depicting homogenous distribution of Cu, O and C within CuO-Cdots-16.6.
TEM images of (a) pristine C-dots and, (b-d) CuO-Cdots-16.6 with inset figure showing corresponding SEAD patterns, (e) EDX spectrum of C-dots.

(a) XRD pattern comparing the change in typical CuO peaks with addition of different C-dots content in reference to pristine C-dots, (b) Raman spectrum depicting variation in typical vibrational frequencies of CuO and C-dots in their hybrid configuration, (c) XPS survey spectra showing increasing C1 and O1s binding energies with different C-dots content, and the deconvoluted profiles for (d) Cu2p, (e) C1s and (f) O1s deduced for CuO-Cdots-16.6.

(a) contact angle measurement showing change in hydrophilicity of CuO post C-dots coating, (b) fluorescence spectra showing decline in typical band intensity of C-dots with physically added different content of CuO (0.0, 0.1 and 0.3 mg) in 10 ml of C-dots dispersion, (c) CV profile depicting the change in redox behaviour of CuO structures post deposition with different C-dots content in standard [Fe(CN)6]3/4− (5 mM) with 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte at a fixed scan rate of 50 mVs−1, (d) variation in CV curves of CuO-Cdot-16.6 with rising scan rate from 50 to 100 mVs−1, and corresponding plot of (e) change in anodic/cathodic current with square root of scan rate, (f) CV response of CuO-Cdot-16.6 against pristine CuO in 0.1 M NaOH consisting 3.5 mM glucose at a fixed scan rate of 50 mVs−1.

(a) CV response of CuO-Cdots-16.6 with different glucose concentration, and corresponding (b) current response linearity against added glucose concentration in range of 3.5 to 5.5 mM, (c) chronoamperometric current variation under fixed potential of 0.5 V for CuO-Cdots-16.6 and CuO showing formers improved current retention during 3500 s.

(a) DPV curves for different glucose concentrations, and the corresponding (b) linear fit calibration depicting suitability of CuO-Cdots-16.6 for glucose concentration, (c) comparison of various ML-models in predicting applicability of the DPV technique in quantifying glucose, and corresponding (d) violin plots showing model’s prediction errors.

(a) amperometric responses for different low-level glucose in range from 15 nM to 225 nM, and corresponding (b) calibration curve depicting the response linearity, (c) amperometric responses showing immunity of the electrode towards common interferents and high sensitivity towards glucose (d) the DFT based molecular stabilization of glucose, CuO lattice and their adduct with corresponding energies and different views(top, and side) depicting the modes of interaction and (e) FMOs (HOMO-LUMO) of glucose, and its ESP maps along with CuO, and its adduct with glucose (carbon atoms are depicted in grey, hydrogen in white, copper in blue, and oxygen of glucose in red and oxygen in CuO surface in orange for more clear visualization). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Detection of spiked glucose level in blood-serum samples using CuO-Cdot-16.6 modified electrode.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Poor conductivity and surface passivation pose critical challenges in metal oxide structures during their application for non-enzymatic oxidation. To address this, we systematically employed in-situ deposition of carbon-quantum dots (C-dots) over copper oxide (CuO), enhancing its electrocatalytic properties for direct non-enzymatic glucose oxidation in alkaline media. The process involved the systematic deposition of varying wt.% of C-dots onto the CuO nanostructure. The electrode’s sensing capability was assessed through CV, DPV, and amperometric measurements, evaluating its suitability in high (0.1 to 0.85 mM) and low glucose concentration levels (15 to 225 nM) with a representative LOD of 1.4 nM (17142.86 µA mM−1 cm−2). Additionally, the CuO-Cdot-16.6 protective coating allowed for long-term working capability, with chronoamperometric measurement confirming a 99 % current retention ability compared to pristine CuO’s 39 % retention during 3500 s of continuous measurement. DFT calculations further confirmed the efficacy of CuO substrate as a scaffold for glucose adsorption. The stable CuO-glucose complex formed due to energetically favorable conditions further strengthens its potential as a sensor. Successful recoveries of spiked glucose serum samples validated the sensor’s practical usage in complex matrices. Moreover, Machine learning was also adopted to validate the accuracy of glucose detection, where artificial neural network (ANN) model emerged as a suitable model to interpret the DPV derived data relationships, adding in sensor working capability and promising its future application in precision/intelligent healthcare.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus is among the top health challenges in the 21st century, with a projection to reach 1.31 billion people affected worldwide by 2050 [1,2]. Diabetes is characterized by the body's incapability to regulate its blood glucose effectively, resulting in life-changing morbidity, sudden yet high mortality, and an increased rate in catching other diseases[3]. These issues reflect the severity of blood glucose regulation and, thus, the need for precise and regular monitoring for medical diagnostics[4]. Since the normal blood glucose level can serve as a definitive parameter for this diagnostic, the challenge in its monitoring lies in exploring suitable methodologies/technologies that are not only efficient and precise but also cost-effective, portable and durable for long-term operation[5]. The development of such technologies holds potential for early diabetes detection and management while also addressing broader health concerns[6]. For instance, accurate glucose monitoring can inform dietary choices and lifestyle changes, thus serving as a preventative measure against obesity—a condition often associated with diabetes[7]. In contrast to conventional lab-based tools (ELISA, enzyme-based detection) and more recently developed glucose monitoring devices[8,9], current research is actively exploring innovative glucose sensing technologies. These advancements are dedicated to far-reaching implications for improving individual health outcomes and mitigating the broader societal impacts of metabolic diseases like diabetes[10].

To achieve, this non-enzymatic metal oxide-based glucose sensors offer a promising avenue owing to their superiority over enzyme-based sensors. Enzymes are sensitive to various factors, whereas metal oxides are abundant worldwide, cost-effective, and feature a simple yet promising configuration on a large scale. They exhibit robust redox-activity capable of oxidizing glucose in considerable concentrations[11,12].

Among many CuO has been a benchmark metal-oxide with history of being investigated for glucose oxidation in different conditions[13,14]. A range of CuO structures, sizes, shapes and morphologies such as wires, flowers and leaves, prepared using diverse synthesis routes like hydrothermal, thermal, and electrochemical have been investigated for their improved oxidation of glucose. The aim is to achieve specificity and sensitivity comparable to enzyme-based counterparts [15,16,17,18,19]. However, among the bottleneck issues, the inherently poor conductivity of CuO (a semi-conductor) and its weaker hydrophilicity play a critical role in its suboptimal and unstable electrochemical performance[20,21,22].

To enhance the inherent performance of CuO, several strategies have been explored, including reducing their size to nano-dimensions and coupling them with conductive substrates such as graphene, MXenes, and carbons[12,23,24]. For instance, Cuara, E., et al.,[25] demonstrated improved electrochemical characteristics of CuO for glucose detection by coupling it with graphene oxide layers. The coupling resulted in a LOD of 0.25 μM (S/N = 3) and a high sensitivity of 845 μA/mM cm2. More recently, a combination of MXene and CuO achieved superior oxidation current with a LOD of 1.96 μM, demonstrating sensors' capability to work in both low (0.004 to 13.3 mM) and high glucose concentrations (15.3 to 28.4 mM), attributed to MXene’s highly conductive support[12]. In another recent development, CuO nanorods were directly grown from a Cu-metal organic framework on a pencil lead electrode. The CuO nanorod modification exhibited high sensitivity (1138.32 mAmM−1 cm−2), and a low LOD (0.11 mM) for glucose in alkaline medium, leveraging the efficient redox capability of Cu/(II)(III) over graphitic surfaces[26].

While the strategies of in-situ growth and scaffold coupling effectively enhance the electrochemical properties of CuO, they face challenges in impacting the commercial viability of metal-oxide-based nonenzymatic glucose sensors. Additionally, the scalability of these approaches seems limited, raising doubt on their potential for integration into portable sensors. This limitation primarily stems from their constrained sensitivity, a critical factor for widespread application and user convenience in glucose monitoring technologies.

As the market for glucose sensors expand, there is growing interest in advancing non-enzymatic platforms that can offer substantial cost benefits through enhanced stability and broader operational temperature ranges. These advantages could significantly reduce the frequency of sensory part replacements and reduce the overall manufacturing costs. However, the advancement of CuO as a non-enzymatic platform, while promising due to its catalytic properties, is mainly hindered by poor conductivity and rapid surface passivation. These limitations compromise the sensitivity of CuO and result in compromised non-enzymatic glucose oxidation performance in alkaline conditions.

In addressing these challenges, modification of CuO with carbon quantum dots (C-dots) can be an efficient and economically viable strategy. C-dots owing to its larger surface to volume ratio[27], coupled with inherent chemical stability[28], biocompatibility, enhanced electric characteristics and ease of surface functionalization can act as a suitability sensitizer compared to its other carbon counterparts i.e. (graphene, or carbon) [29,30,31]. Moreover, C-dots could be derived from multiple cost-effective and non-toxic precursors, enabling performance enhancement with ecological benefits[32]. Thus, the integration of C-dots with CuO through an optimized in-situ modification process would not only improve latter’s overall surface characteristics but may also act as a protective coat that prevents the rapid surface passivation of the CuO in alkaline environments. This protective modification can be anticipated to significantly enhance the stability of the CuO-platform, thereby extending the constructed sensor's operational lifespan.

Herein, we propose a simple and efficient strategy involving direct hydrothermal growth of C-dots onto CuO nanosheets using ascorbic acid as a precursor to enhance its surface characteristics and mitigate electrode passivation issues during its utilization as non-enzymatic electrocatalyst for glucose oxidation in alkaline conditions. Systematic experiments demonstrate that an increase in C-dots content on CuO significantly elevates its electrocatalytic activity. Specifically, the CuO-Cdots composite with 16.6 % C-dots content realized a twofold enhancement in current and a reduced potential difference for glucose oxidation in alkaline media. Furthermore, this composite showed exceptional electrode stability, maintaining 99 % of its current over prolonged periods, surpassing the performance of pristine CuO. Amperometric detection demonstrated sensitive glucose detection capability ranging from 15 nM to 225 nM with a low LOD of 1.4 nM, without interference from common biomolecules. Moreover, density functional theory (DFT) calculations corroborated the enhanced interaction and stability of the CuO-glucose complex. Furthermore, the DPV based sensing was translated by different ML models such as Linear Regression, Random Forest, LightGBM, and Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), where ANN model demonstrated minimal error matrices (MSE = 0.0142, MAE = 0.0142, and RMSE = 0.0179) validating its relatively higher superiority for predictive assessment of the developed non-enzymatic glucose sensors. The practical application of the sensor was validated through the accurate detection of glucose in real blood serum samples, demonstrating its potential for real-world diagnostic applications.

Copper (II) chloride dihydrate (CuCl2⋅2H2O),gallic acid(C7H6O5), sodium hydroxide(NaOH), glucose(C₆H12O6), galactose(C6H12O6), fructose(C6H12O6), lactose(C12H22O11), sucrose(C12H22O11), ascorbic acid(C6H8O6), uric acid(C5H4N4O3), dopamine(C8H11NO2), and bovine serum albumin (BSA)were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). A set of glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) (0.07 cm2) were obtained from CHI-instruments, UK along with polishing kits and 0.05 μm polishing alumina. The morphological evaluation was carried using field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Quanta FEG 250, FEI, United States) equipped with EDS and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, Talos 200 s, FEI, USA). The compositional characteristics were evaluated using X-ray diffraction (XRD, P-analytical X’ Pert powder, Netherlands), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB 250, Thermo Scientific, United States). Raman analysis was carried using HORIBA Jobin-Yvon LabRAM HR800 confocal Raman microscope. Electrochemical measurements were carried using a potentiostat model E-760 (CH Instruments, Texas, USA) with a three-electrode system. The modified electrodes were used as working, while Ag/AgCl and platinum (Pt) wire acted as reference and counter-electrode respectively.

The CuO structures were prepared using conventional and our previously reported hydrothermal route[22,33,34,35,36,37]. In a typical batch, 1 mM CuCl2⋅2H2O and 0.3 g of gallic acid (GA) was homogenized in de-ionized water for 30 min under constant stirring conditions. The mixture was then added 5 mM of NaOH (5 ml) and stirred for additional 20 min. Subsequently, the alkaline mixture was transferred into a hydrothermal reactor and subjected to heat treatment in a pre-heated electrical oven for 24 h at a temperature of 120 °C.

The prepared sheet-like clusters (black-colored) were collected following the colling of the reactor and the materials were thoroughly washed with de-ionized water and ethanol several time to ensure removal of un-reacted salts and template molecules. In this reaction, GA served as a soft-template to control the size and morphology of the formed CuO nanostructures. The formed clusters were then dried in an electrical oven at 60 °C overnight before being used as a scaffold for the direct growth of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ deposition of carbon dots over pre-prepared CuO sheet-like clusters
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, a specific weight (5 g) of CuO was dispersed in a 1 M ascorbic acid solution in 30 mL of de-aerated de-ionized water. Ascorbic acid served as a carbon source and CuO acted as a scaffold for carbon nucleation and growth [38]. The mixture was stirred for approximately 1 h before undergoing hydrothermal treatment in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel vessel at 250 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the CuO nanosheets were separated using a simple filtration process and dried in an oven at 60 °C before being utilized as a catalyst for electrocatalytic glucose oxidation.

To ensure abundant deposition of C-dots, the concentration of ascorbic acid was varied in the range of 0.2, 0.5, and 1.0 M. The deposited content of C-dots was evaluated from EDX analysis of the samples. Table S1 shows the various C-dot content deposited against different concentrations of ascorbic acid. Concentrations of 0.2, 0.5, and 1 mM enabled 2.5, 8.0, and 16.6 wt% deposition of C-dots, respectively. The different wt.% of C-dots on CuO is referred to according to the formula; CuO-Cdot-X, where X is the % of C-dots, i.e., CuO-Cdot-2.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic ink preparation, electrode construction of CuO and C-dots-CuO samples and their electrochemical testing
ce:section.ce:para: To convert the synthesized CuO and its C-dot decorated composites into viable inks for electrode modification, a quantity of 6.5 mg of CuO-Cdot-X samples was initially ground to ensure uniformity of the powder. This was subsequently dispersed in a 5 mL mixture of isopropanol and Nafion solution (3:1 v/v ratio, with Nafion at 5 wt%) to create a slurry. The slurry underwent ultrasonic mixing before being applied to pre-cleaned glassy carbon electrodes for electrochemical testing. The volume of the catalyst ink applied varied from 2 to 8 μL to fine-tune the electrochemical response measured against a 3.5 mM glucose solution in 0.1 M NaOH. For comparison, pristine CuO electrodes were fabricated using same method to confirm that the observed electrochemical performance was indeed due to the inclusion of C-dots.

Electrochemical measurements were conducted using a 3-electrode setup, comprising a platinum wire counter electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode in saturated KCl, and the modified CuO and CuO-Cdot-X electrodes as working electrodes. The electrochemical assessment was carried using cyclic voltammetry (CV) within a potential range of 0 to 0.75 V in 0.1 M NaOH electrolyte. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was conducted with a step potential of 2 mV and a pulse amplitude of 25 mV, while chronoamperometric tests were carried out at a constant potential of 0.5 V and a glucose concentration of 2.5 mM over a duration of 3500 s.
The details of computational analysis and machine learning models is provided in supplementary section.

A simply wet-chemical strategy was adopted to deposit C-dots (in-situ) over hydrothermally grown CuO nanosheet-like structures to overcome the inherent charge-transfer limitations of CuO for non-enzymatic glucose sensing (Fig. 1 ).

Fig. 2 shows the HR-SEM images of CuO NSs and its C-dot coated counterpart (CuO-Cdots-16.6) exhibiting typical sheet-like morphology stacked together forming an array of randomly stacked dices. The corresponding EDS spectra of pristine CuO is given as Figure S1 exhibiting typical energy peaks related to Cu and O without carbon. Table S1 shows the EDX derived wt.% of the elements for CuO-Cdot-X samples. The gradually increasing wt.% of C-dots was achieved with rising concentration of precursor solution (ascorbic acid) suggesting that nucleation of C-dots was directly related to precursor concentration. In this case, CuO-Cdot-16.6 was selected as representative system due to its relatively abundant C-dots content and superior electrochemical characteristics. Fig. 2 (b) displays HR-SEM images of CuO-Cdots-16.6, depicting an array of homogenously sized CuO sheets with average width of 50–75 nm ± 1.5 nm. The high-resolution image (Fig. 2 (c-d)) reveals the deposition of C-dots homogenously around each sheet, confirming their intimate contact with CuO surface. In Fig. 2 (f), colored EDS mapping of CuO-Cdots-16.6 further confirmed the uniform distribution of Cu, O and C elements. The complete coverage of C-dots, in this case, was directly attributed to in-situ approach, enabling an intimate heterostructure interface formation suitable for rapid and efficient charge-transfer processes during surface based redox reaction.

TEM analysis was carried to further assess the phase-formation and interfacial connection between CuO and C-dots. In Fig. 3
(a), TEM images of pristine C-dots reveal a uniform spherical shape with an average diameter of 10–15 nm. The formed C-dots exhibited a typical amorphous nature, as depicted from its SEAD pattern (inset Fig. 1 (a)) and thus, their deposition on CuO was anticipated to improve the diffusion of glucose molecules towards the electrode, enabling higher signal sensitivity. Fig. 3 (b-c) shows the TEM images of CuO-Cdots-16.6, with sheet like structural features of CuO composed of nanoparticles with an average size of 25–90 nm ± 1.4 nm. The inset Fig. 3 (c) shows the typical SEAD pattern confirming the lattice fringes 110, 111, 202 indexed to monoclinic phase CuO [39,40], reflecting the crystalline nature of CuO within CuO-Cdots-16.6. Fig. 3 (d) shows TEM image depicting the interface formation between CuO and C-dots, with overlapping structural features reflecting the in-situ growth of C-dots over CuO NSs. The EDX spectrum of pristine C-dots is provided as Fig. 3 (e) as a reference.

To further evaluate the changes in compositional characteristics of CuO post in-situ deposition, XRD and Raman analyses were conducted with reference to pristine CuO NSs. Fig. 4
(a) shows the XRD pattern with typical peaks for pristine CuO at (110), (002), (111), (202), (020), (202), (113), (311), (220), (311), and (222), indexed to the monoclinic phase of CuO as referenced against JCSPD 05-0661[36,37,41]. No other peaks related to CuO and hydroxide phases were identified, confirming the purity of formed CuO NSs. In case of CuO-Cdot-X, the peak-profile exhibited a similar pattern with an additional shoulder-like peak near 2-theta 2θ°, confirming the presence of amorphous carbon with CuO NSs. The gradually rising shoulder peak of carbon from CuO-Cdot-2.5 to CuO-Cdots-16.6 with proportionally declining CuO peaks further confirmed the increasing coverage of C-dots over CuO NSs[42]. Since, the C-dots were amorphous in nature, their interaction with CuO was further evaluated using Raman analysis. Fig. 4 (b) shows the Raman spectra for CuO and its C-dot-covered counterparts. The Raman spectrum of the CuO NSs exhibits characteristic bands (Fig. 4 (b)) near 275 cm−1 related to the Ag mode, considered an active optical phonon mode of the monoclinic (C2/c) CuO phase [43]. The presence of a strong Ag mode with additional weak B1g and B 2g modes near 327 cm−1 and 612 cm−1 confirmed crystalline nature of CuO within monoclinic symmetry [44]. In the case of CuO-Cdot-X, a rising D and G bands near 1350 and 1650 cm−1 was observed with a change of C-dots deposition content from 2.5 to 16.6 %. In the case of carbon, the ID and IG ratio provide significant insights into the defect content or degree of graphitization, which can have a significant influence on the overall electrochemical characteristics of the final composite[45,46].

XPS analysis was carried to further assess the change in the electronic state of CuO post in-situ growth of C-dots. Fig. 4 (c) shows the survey spectra depicting typical Cu, O and C peaks within CuO-Cdot-X hybrids. Unlike pristine CuO, which only exhibited Cu and O peaks, a gradually rising C peak was evident in the case of CuO-Cdot-X samples with an increase of C-dot content from 2.5 to 16.6 %. The deconvoluted high-resolution Cu2p, C1s, and O1s binding energy profiles for CuO-Cdot-16.6 were then compared to pristine CuO to confirm the binding energy shifts. Fig. 4 (d) shows the Cu 2p fittings where typical Cu2p 3/2 and Cu2p 1/2 peaks were determined near 934.4 and 954. 6 eV along with representative satellite peaks near 941.7 and 962.4 eV, confirming the electronic state of Cu as Cu (II) within CuO[51]. The C-dots counterpart (CuO-Cdots-16.6) exhibited a similar peak profile, however with corresponding peak intensities relatively weaker than its pristine counterpart, suggesting that C-dots have successfully covered the surface. The C1s spectra of pristine C-dots were compared with that obtained for CuO-Cdot-16.6. Fig. 4 (e) shows the fitting profile exhibiting C–C, C = O and O = C-OH peaks at 284.4, 286.1 and 288.6 eV respectively[52]. In the case of CuO-Cdot-16.6, a similar peak profile was observed, however with declined intensities and a slightly low-energy shifted O = C-OH peak from 288.7 to 288.3, suggesting interaction of C-dots' functional groups with the CuO scaffold[53]. The O1s binding energy peaks were deconvoluted for lattice Cu–O, oxygen vacancies or oxygen from COOH group of C-dots, and adsorbed oxygen from –OH group in the case of pristine CuO (Fig. 4 (f)). In the case of CuO-Cdots-16.6, despite similar peak profile, an increased peak intensity near 531.3 eV and 534.2 eV, related to lattice or functional group oxygen (COOH), was observed, confirming the supplementary oxygen content in CuO-Cdots-16.6 and presence of sufficient C-dots over CuO to facilitate their surface wettability[7]. The presence of C-dots with hydrophilic groups over CuO was anticipated to improve the wetting ability of CuO NSs. Fig. 5
(a) shows the contact angle measurement for pristine CuO and CuO-Cdots-16.6, where a significant reduction for the former from 121° to 66° confirmed the improved wettability of CuO surfaces. This is critical in terms of the surface electrochemical processes, as increased wettability facilitates greater electrolytic diffusion by improving electrode–electrolyte contact, thereby promoting a more efficient interfacial redox process[54]. The interaction between the C-dots and CuO was further corroborated from the standpoint of the C-dots. In Fig. 5 (b), the fluorescence emission spectra of the C-dots in a wavelength range of 200 to 900 nm shows a noticeable reduction in intensity upon the incremental physical addition of CuO nanostructures[55]. Specifically, the addition of 0.1 mg of CuO resulted in a 47 % decrease in fluorescence intensity, while a higher addition of 0.3 mg led to a more significant reduction of 78 %. This supports the effectiveness of CuO in adsorbing C-dots, further corroborating their suitability to act as a scaffold for the in-situ growth of C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced electrochemical characteristics of C-dots decorated CuO for oxidation of glucose
ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the impact of in-situ synthesized C-dots on the electrocatalytic performance of CuO, CV was used to investigate the alterations in the redox behavior of CuO electrodes[56,57]. Fig. 5 (c) shows the CV profiles for CuO electrodes modified with varying concentrations of C-dots (2.5 %, 8.0 %, and 16.6 %) in a standard redox probe of [Fe(CN)6]3/4− (5 mM) with 0.1 M KCl as the supporting electrolyte. Across all configurations, the electrodes exhibited quasi-reversible redox behavior, characterized by distinct peak shapes and currents. In contrast to pristine CuO ( Figure S4 ), which displayed a poor current response and a peak-to-peak separation (ΔE) of 0.347 V, indicative of sluggish kinetics due to the limited conductivity inherent to the metal-oxide framework, the Cdot-modified samples demonstrated notably lower ΔE values. Specifically, a progressive decrease in ΔE to 0.31, 0.26, and 0.21 V, with increasing C-dots content of 2.5 %, 8.0 %, and 16.6 %, respectively, suggested improved electron transfer rates owing to enhanced conductivity and surface-active sites. Since CuO-Cdot-16.6 realized the highest redox current with the smallest ΔE, the EASA of the corresponding electrode was further deduced using the modified Randles-Sevcik equation (1), following the variation of redox-peak current against increasing scan rates from 50 to 100 mVs−1 [58,59].

For the standard redox probe ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4−), D is 7.6 × 10−6 cm2/s, n is 1, and Ip is the anodic current response, and C is the concentration (5 × 10−3 M).

The observed variation in redox current corresponding to increasing scan rates for CuO-Cdot-16.6 and pristine CuO is depicted in Fig. 5 (d) and Figure S5 . The average EASA of the CuO-Cdot-16.6 electrode was calculated to be 1.013 × 10−5 cm2, which was approximately five times greater than that of pristine CuO (2.083 × 10−6 cm2). This substantial increase corroborated the contribution of C-dots to enhancing the number of accessible active sites for catalysis. Furthermore, it is evident that the pristine CuO electrode, owing to its poor conductivity, suffers significant electron transfer limitation, a typical challenge for metal oxides[60], as depicted by the redox peaks shift toward higher potentials with a rising scan rate ( Figure S5 ). In contrast, the CuO-Cdots-16.6 electrode demonstrated a more stable profile with minimal peak shifts, affirming enhanced kinetic performance and suitability for operation at elevated scan rates (Fig. 5 (d-e)). This enhancement can be directly attributed to the C-dots deposition, which acts as a protective layer, mitigating the rapid passivation of CuO and thereby ameliorating its inherent charge-transfer limitations during surface redox reaction.

Once the electrochemical superiority of the hybrid electrode was confirmed, the performance of CuO-Cdot-16.6 was evaluated in an alkaline medium containing a constant concentration of 3.5 mM glucose. The CuO-Cdot-16.6 electrode (Fig. 5 (f)) showed a notably enhanced anodic peak current, registering 148.7 μA at 0.51 V, in contrast to the 26.4 μA at 0.57 V observed for the pristine CuO electrode. This 17.7-fold increase in the anodic current for glucose oxidation with the CuO-Cdot-16.6 electrode, achieved at a marginally lower overpotential, can be ascribed to the synergistic effects of the improved surface charge-transferability and the proliferation of surface-active sites afforded by the in-situ synthesized C-dots. The quantum dots not only expedite the surface redox reactions but also significantly improve the charge transfer at the electrode–electrolyte interface, thereby substantially amplifying the glucose oxidation signal[61]. The response of the electrode was evaluated for different concentrations of glucose in the range of 3.5 to 5.5 mM, which is within the physiological blood glucose range (Fig. 6
(a))[62]. The proportionally rising anodic peak current exhibited excellent linearity, where the concentration-current relationship could be defined as Ip anodic = 82.18 ± 4.388 (μA/mM)-143.09 ± 10.3482 μA (R2 = 0.9952) (Fig. 6 (b)). The inset Fig. 6 (a) shows a simple illustration reflecting the oxidation of glucose over the CuO framework. In a typical alkaline environment, glucose molecules tend to absorb on CuO modified electrode, facilitated, in this case, by the in-situ-grown C-dots, promoting the adsorption of glucose molecules. Under the influence of the potential, the Cu(II) species changes to Cu(III), oxidizing glucose to gluconolactone via the formation of a keto group through the dehydrogenation of glucose by –OH− ions[25]. This oxidation, accompanied by electron transfer from glucose, is mediated by the redox couple Cu (II)/(III), and facilitated by C-dots, enabling a robust anodic signal response near 0.5 V. The following equations explain this mechanism:

The role of C-dots in this case is multifaceted. The direct deposition of C-dots not only facilitated the overall charge conductivity, promoting the interfacial charge-transfer process, but also provided more active sites, enabling greater molecular engagement with the electrode and, thus, higher sensitivity. At the same time, C-dots over CuO also acts as a proactive coating, ensuring consistent electrode activity and, thus, the electrode's long-term applicability of the electrodes for practical and continuous operation. Fig. 6 (c) compares the chronoamperometric current generation ability of CuO and its CuO-Cdot-16.6 counterpart under a fixed potential of 0.5 V and a fixed glucose concentration (2.5 mM). During continuous measurement for 3500 s, the pristine CuO, despite its ability to generate oxidation current, exhibited a continuously declining trend, a direct consequence of its rapid surface passivation[63]. The current retention was deduced between 1000 and 3500 s, where pristine CuO could only retain 39 % of its initial current, further confirming its inability for long-term working. In contrast, CuO-Cdot-16.6, owing to its protected surface, showed a stable response with 99 % current retention during the course of measurement, corroborating the suitability of C-dots as an efficient protector for metal oxide surfaces.

To further evaluate the suitability of the devised electrode for a wider glucose concentration range, DPV measurement-based calibration was performed due to its superior sensitivity over CV[64]. Fig. 7
(a) shows gradually rising DPV curves for a wider glucose concentration range from 0.1 to 8.5 mM, with a set interval of 0.1 mM. The concentration–response relationship was defined as IpDPV = 112.50 ± 8.163 (μA/mM) + 227.8 ± 5.946 (μA) (R2 = 0.9714) with an estimated LOD of 0.06 mM (S/N = 3) (Fig. 7 (b)).

The integration of electrochemical sensors into diverse technology-driven applications has led to the need for sophisticated data-analysis methods[65]. Among all, machine learning (ML) has only recently started to gain recognition in electrochemical sensors and is anticipated to play a critical role in advancing implantable future sensor systems for continuous monitoring and identification of trends to improve overall health management[66]. Among various techniques, DPV, owing to the high-signal-to-noise ratio and time-resolved nature of the signal, can be easily transformed into structured data feasible for computational analysis with various ML algorithms for improving predictive modeling of the sensor performance[67]. In this case, the DPV signals generated for glucose oxidation (Fig. 7 (a)) were categorized as meaningful attributes from the DPV curves (details in supplementary data) followed by ML modeling for predictive assessment of detected concentration against generated current DPV signal outcome. Linear regression, random forest, lightGBM, and artificial neural networks (ANN) were applied to predict analyte concentrations based on input DPV current. The models were evaluated using Leave-One-Out cross-validation, focusing on error metrics like MSE, MAE, RMSE, and R2. Fig. 7 (c) shows the plot depicting the predictive accuracy of the adopted models against actual DPV results. This provides insights into the capabilities and limitations of each model, indicating their suitability for the complex dynamics of the electrochemical data. In general, linear regression demonstrated high precision with an MSE of 0.0009, MAE of 0.0274, RMSE of 0.0308, and R2 of 0.9831, highlighting its suitability for robust predictive detection. While the Random Forest model was usable, it demonstrated less precision with an MSE of 0.0083, MAE of 0.0672, RMSE of 0.0911, and R2 of 0.8524, suggesting possible overfitting. LightGBM with an MSE of 0.01, MAE of 0.1, RMSE of 0.1, and R2 of 0.822 implied difficulty in fitting the DPV data variability. In contrast, artificial neural networks (ANN) exhibit the best fit with an MSE of 0.0141, MAE of 0.0142, RMSE of 0.0179, and an R2 of 0.994, showcasing exceptional ability in modeling complex, non-linear relationships within the data for precise concentration predictions. The ANN model’s exceptional fit for modeling the non-linear complexities of DPV data offers a promising future for these sensors, especially in the context of continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) systems for future implantable sensors. Its predictive accuracy and adaptive learning capabilities hold the potential for handling large health-related data, aiding in individual health diagnosis. The violin plot (Fig. 7 (d)) further visualizes the range and distribution of predicted errors for the adopted models. The split design of the violin plot reflects the density of data points, providing a comprehensive comparison in identifying the most suitable model to assess glucose sensing data from DPV. Figure S6 provides a dual-axis chart that offers a balanced view of the error reduction and the corresponding model fits. The juxtaposed error metrics (MSE, MAE, RMSE) against the coefficient of determination (R2) illustrate a trade-off between minimizing error and achieving a good fit. The minimal error observed for ANN further confirmed the suitability of this model for interpreting DPV signals.

Due to the high adsorption ability of C-dots[68], coupled with the redox-ability of CuO, the current generation per unit glucose concentration is exceptionally high. Consequently, glucose detection was evaluated in extremely low concentration ranges, specifically from 15 to 225 nM. The detection was carried out using the amperometric method under stirring conditions (600 rpm) with 0.1 M NaOH as the electrolyte. The sensing was performed under a fixed potential of 0.5 V, where pre-diluted glucose was injected at fixed intervals to assess the current. Fig. 8
(a) shows the I vs t graph with sharp spikes indicating the added glucose concentration. The sharp peak current reflected the robust capability of CuO-Cdots-16.6 to rapidly oxidize glucose, facilitating a highly sensitive and rapid response to added glucose. Fig. 8 (b) shows the corresponding calibration depicting the sensor's excellent working linearity in extremely low concentrations of glucose. The concentration-current relationship for CuO-Cdots-16 was deduced from the amperometric calibration and could be defined via the following equation: Ipamp = 0.00120 ± 0.00004 (μA/μM) (GLU) + 12.192± (μA) (R2 = 0.9904). The limit of detection for the set concentration window (15 to 225 nM) was determined to be1.4 nM (S/N = 3), which is relatively lower than most of the reported sensors designed for glucose detection using diverse materials and different methods ( Table S2 ). Fig. 8 (c) shows the amperometric response of the electrode in solution spiked with different interferent molecules such as galactose, fructose, lactose, sucrose, ascorbic acid, uric acid, dopamine, and large molecules like bovine serum albumin in a fixed concentration (15 nM). As seen, the addition of interferents has a negligible response, with the exception of sucrose, which produced a slight current response. However, the response is not significantly large enough to be considered as interference. Most interferents only caused minor current variation, whereas pronounced spikes were only evident for glucose, confirming the electrode's high selectivity towards glucose in alkaline conditions at a fixed potential of 0.5 V.

To visualize glucose's interaction with the modified electrode (CuO-Cdot-16.6), DFT calculations were performed to examine glucose adsorption on the CuO unit cell surface. The DFT-optimized geometries of glucose and the CuO structure are depicted in Fig. 8 (d), with the CuO-glucose complex stabilized at a lower energy level of −83054.875 Ha. This contrasts with the isolated CuO at −82367.961 Ha and glucose at −687.353 Ha, indicating a thermodynamically favorable interaction. The adsorption of glucose onto CuO was determined to be mediated by non-covalent interactions, including hydrogen bonds between glucose hydroxyl groups and CuO surface oxygens, as well as van der Waals forces between the surface copper atoms and glucose oxygen atoms. To delve deeper into the nature of these interactions, a HOMO-LUMO analysis was conducted (Fig. 8 (e)). For glucose, the HOMOs were concentrated around the oxygen atoms, indicating their nucleophilic character with an energy level of −0.217 Ha. Conversely, the LUMOs, with an energy level of 0.01344 Ha, were primarily located near hydrogen atoms, confirming their electrophilic nature. Electrostatic potential (ESP) maps further elucidate the electron distribution within the molecules and their complex. CuO's multicolored surface and glucose's pink hues denote regions of high electron density, while the CuO-glucose adduct revealed a composite electron density landscape resulting from the binding interaction. Notably, the oxygen atoms in both glucose and CuO exhibit a higher electron density, whereas a positive charge density is predominantly associated with the glucose's carbon and hydrogen atoms, as well as the copper atoms on the CuO surface. This indicates that the hydrogen–oxygen and oxygen-copper interactions are critical for the adduct formation. The ESP of the CuO-glucose adduct suggests that the binding was facilitated by the alignment of glucose's electron-rich regions (visualized in a yellowish tint) with the electron-deficient areas on CuO (in a bluish shade). Additionally, the interaction of glucose's charge-dense hydrogen and carbon atoms with the electron-rich copper atoms on CuO leads to a dispersion of charge density throughout the adduct. These interactions between glucose and CuO were further substantiated by alterations in the electronic state distribution and energy levels within the adduct. The density of states (DOS) profiles for glucose, CuO, and their composite indicated a subtle shift in the DOS spectrum of CuO, particularly within the s-, p-, and d-orbitals, following glucose binding, confirming the involvement of orbitals in adduct formation ( Figure S7 ). Furthermore, a heightened DOS intensity in the low-energy region (−0.2 to −0.1 Ha) post adsorption suggests that stable electrons within this energy domain are participating in the adsorption via non-bonding interactions.

To assess the practical applicability of the developed electrode, real-blood glucose testing was conducted using the DPV-based methodology. Blood glucose levels were determined from pre-diluted serum samples that spiked with a known quantity of glucose. The recovery percentage signified the electrode's precision for real-time analysis. Blood samples obtained with informed consent from healthy volunteers had their initial glucose levels established using a standard glucometer. Subsequently, the samples underwent centrifugation to collect the serum, which was then systematically diluted with 0.1 M NaOH to facilitate the testing process. The serum samples were spiked with known glucose concentration and subjected to the developed DPV protocol due to its heightened sensitivity in detecting elevated glucose levels to understand the matrix effect on direct non-enzymatic glucose determination. The standard-addition method was adopted to recover the known concentration of glucose following the developed calibration (Fig. 7 (b)). As indicated in Table 1 , the electrode consistently achieved accurate glucose recovery from the spiked samples, demonstrating a satisfactory RSD% value and validating its efficacy within complex biological matrices.

In summary, a simple strategy involving the in-situ growth of C-dots over copper oxide (CuO) nanostructures was employed to address the inherent challenges of metal-oxides, such as poor conductivity and electrode passivation during non-enzymatic glucose oxidation in alkaline media. The approach involved a 2-step procedure: first, hydrothermal growth of CuO nanoarray-like structures using gallic acid (GA) as a template, followed by the in-situ formation of C-dots using an ascorbic acid precursor under high-temperature hydrothermal conditions. The deposition content of C-dots was systematically varied using different concentrations of the precursor (0.2, 0.5, and 1 mM), resulting in 2.5, 8.0, and 16.6 wt% deposition of C-dots over CuO nanostructure, respectively. The influence of in-situ deposited C-dots over the electrochemical characteristics of CuO enabled the establishment of a structure–activity relationship, where the increasing content of C-dots demonstrated enhanced oxidation of glucose at a relatively low-over potential (0.5 V) compared to pristine CuO. In a systematic comparison, CuO-Cdot-16.6 exhibited a high current response of 148.7 μA at 0.5 V, in comparison to 26.4 μA at 0.57 V for pristine CuO in alkaline media. The sensing capability of the electrode was investigated using DPV and amperometric measurements with both high (0.1 to 0.85 mM) and low glucose concentration levels (15 to 225 nM) with a deduced LOD of 1.4 nM using amperometry. The improved signal and higher sensitivity were directly attributed to the coating of C-dots, which integrated higher conductivity, facilitating charge transfer at the electrode–electrolyte interface. Moreover, the deposition of C-dots enhanced the electrode's long-term working capability. Chronoamperometric measurements under a fixed potential confirmed a 99 % current retention ability of the CuO-Cdot-16.6, compared to 39 % retention of CuO during 3500 s of continuous measurement. Furthermore, the incorporation of ML to corroborate the accuracy of glucose detection on DPV further confirmed ANN as a suitable model for discerning complex data relationships. This enrichment in sensor performance holds promise for future advancements in precision diagnostics and intelligent healthcare solutions. DFT-based calculations further confirmed the suitability of CuO as a suitable scaffold for adsorption of glucose based on favorable interaction and a stable CuO-glucose adduct. Moreover, the practicality of the sensor was demonstrated by its successful (99–102 %) utilization in detecting spiked blood glucose from real-blood sera samples. The proposed strategy presents a straightforward and cost-effective solution for addressing the inherent electrochemical limitations of metal oxide structures, facilitating their commercial application in non-enzymatic glucose sensing.
